abstract:
While the shift from mandatory service to all-volunteer forces characterized the European military landscape for decades, a deteriorating security environment has recently forced many nations to bring conscription back to the table. Modern conscription systems, however, are not mere replicas of the past; they have been significantly reshaped by contemporary neoliberal values and a heightened emphasis on individual agency. This study examines the evolution and continued viability of the conscription model across six European countries: Estonia, Finland, Lithuania, Norway, Sweden, and Switzerland. While four of these nations have continuously maintained their systems, Lithuania and Sweden have recently reinstated them to address new strategic realities. 

By analyzing the unique historical and geopolitical contexts of these cases, the study identifies three critical dimensions that define the modern conscription era: the proportion of the birth cohort called to serve, the integration of personal motivation into recruitment, and the shifting approaches to female participation. Integrating these factors, the article proposes a new typology consisting of three distinct conscription models. Our findings demonstrate that rather than being an outdated relic of the Cold War, conscription has survived by adapting to the values of modern society and the complexities of the current European security landscape.
